questions.q: Which of the following is **not** part of the definition of an étale morphism of schemes?
choices: flat
unramified
smooth of relative dimension 0
proper
questions.explain: Étale = flat + unramified (equivalently: smooth of relative dimension $0$). Properness is a separate condition; étale morphisms are by definition of finite presentation but need not be proper.

questions.q: Consider the map $f\colon \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}\to \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$, $z\mapsto z^5$. Away from the origin it is étale. Over a point $t\neq 0$, how many geometric points does the fibre have?
questions.explain: The fibre over $t\neq 0$ is $\{z : z^5 = t\}$, i.e. $5$ distinct fifth roots, so the étale degree is $5$. Over $t=0$ the fibre collapses to a single ramified point $z=0$.

questions.q: The map $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]$ sending $t\mapsto t^n$ is étale exactly when:
choices: always
$n$ is coprime to $p$
$n$ is a power of $p$
never
questions.explain: The derivative $nt^{n-1}$ must be invertible on $\mathbb{G}_m$; over $\mathbb{F}_p$ that holds iff $p\nmid n$. Otherwise the map is inseparable (Frobenius-like) and ramified.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying a morphism $f:X\to Y$ is étale using the infinitesimal lifting criterion?
choices: Show $f$ is flat, then check the diagonal $\Delta_{X/Y}$ is an open immersion, then conclude unramifiedness.
Check unramifiedness (diagonal is open immersion), then check flatness, then conclude étaleness.
Check smoothness of the total space, then restrict to relative dimension $0$ fibres, then verify properness.
Verify $\Omega^1_{X/Y}=0$ (unramified), then verify liftings of nilpotent thickenings are unique (formally étale), then check finite presentation.
hard.explain: The standard verification: $\Omega^1_{X/Y}=0$ detects unramifiedness; unique lifting of $T\hookrightarrow T'$ (any nilpotent thickening) gives formal étaleness; adding finite presentation gives étaleness. Flatness follows automatically from formal étaleness + finite presentation.

hard.q: Let $f:\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[t]]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[s]]$ be given by $s\mapsto t^2$. This map is **not** étale at the closed point. Which failure is the direct obstruction?
choices: Failure of flatness: $\mathbb{C}[[t]]$ is not a flat $\mathbb{C}[[s]]$-module.
Failure of unramifiedness: $\Omega^1_{\mathbb{C}[[t]]/\mathbb{C}[[s]]}\neq 0$ because the residue field extension $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{C}$ is inseparable.
Failure of unramifiedness: $\Omega^1_{\mathbb{C}[[t]]/\mathbb{C}[[s]]}\neq 0$ because $ds=2t\,dt$ vanishes mod $t$, so $dt$ is a free nonzero differential.
Failure of finite presentation: power series rings are not finitely presented.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{C}[[t]]$ is flat over $\mathbb{C}[[s]]$ (torsion-free over a DVR). The residue fields are both $\mathbb{C}$, so separability of the extension is not the issue. The obstruction is that $\Omega^1_{\mathbb{C}[[t]]/\mathbb{C}[[s]]}$ is generated by $dt$ with relation $2t\,dt=0$; since $2t$ is not a unit (it lies in the maximal ideal), $dt\neq 0$ and the module is nonzero — the map is ramified.

hard.q: The Artin–Schreier map $\wp:\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}\to\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}$, $x\mapsto x^2-x$, is a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor (hence étale). How many geometric points lie over $0\in\mathbb{A}^1(\overline{\mathbb{F}_2})$?
hard.explain: The fibre over $0$ is $\{x\in\overline{\mathbb{F}_2}: x^2-x=0\}=\{x(x-1)=0\}=\{0,1\}$, giving $2$ geometric points. This confirms the étale degree is $p=2$ and the torsor is everywhere unramified.

questions.q: Why do we take the prime $\ell$ distinct from the characteristic $p$ of the ground field?
choices: Because $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p)$ sees only $p$-torsion and is badly behaved.
Because working with $\ell = p$ collapses étale cohomology to singular cohomology.
There is no such restriction; any prime works.
Because Frobenius has no action when $\ell = p$.
questions.explain: In characteristic $p$, the étale cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p^n)$ is pathological (one uses crystalline cohomology instead). For $\ell \neq p$ the theory is well-behaved and gives the right Betti numbers.

questions.q: Let $E$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^1_{\text{ét}}(E, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $g = 3$?
questions.explain: For a smooth projective curve of genus $g$, $\dim H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = 2g$. With $g=3$ this is $6$ — matching the classical Betti number $b_1 = 2g$ for a complex curve.

choices: $\varinjlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n) \otimes \mathbb{Q}$
$(\varprojlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell} \mathbb{Q}_\ell$
$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Q})$ directly
$\bigoplus_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$
questions.explain: One takes the inverse limit over torsion sheaves $\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$ to land in $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-modules, then tensors with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ to get a finite-dimensional $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space.

hard.q: Why does the naive colimit $\varinjlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$ fail to give a useful $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space, and what goes wrong?
choices: The colimit is always zero because the transition maps are zero.
The colimit gives a $\mathbb{Z}[1/\ell]$-module, not a $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module, so the $\ell$-adic topology is lost.
The colimit is not functorial in $X$.
The colimit gives a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space independent of $\ell$, making it useless for distinguishing primes.
hard.explain: The direct limit inverts $\ell$ and yields a $\mathbb{Z}[1/\ell]$-module, which loses the $\ell$-adic topology and completeness structure. The inverse limit $\varprojlim_n H^i(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$ instead gives a profinite $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module; tensoring with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ then yields the correct finite-dimensional $\ell$-adic cohomology.

hard.q: Let $X = \mathbb{P}^3_{\overline{\mathbb{F}_q}}$. Using $\dim H^{2k}_{\text{ét}}(\mathbb{P}^n,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=1$ for $0\leq k\leq n$ and $0$ otherwise, compute $\sum_{i=0}^{6}\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$.
hard.explain: For $\mathbb{P}^3$ the nonzero cohomology groups are $H^0,H^2,H^4,H^6$, each of dimension $1$. All odd-degree groups vanish. The total is $1+1+1+1=4$.

hard.q: Which chain of constructions correctly assembles the Tate module $T_\ell(E)$ of an elliptic curve $E$ over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ (with $\ell\neq p$) into $H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$?
choices: $T_\ell(E)=\varinjlim_n E[\ell^n]$, then $V_\ell(E)=T_\ell(E)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell}\mathbb{Q}_\ell\cong H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)^\vee$
$T_\ell(E)=\varprojlim_n E[\ell^n]$, then $V_\ell(E)=T_\ell(E)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell}\mathbb{Q}_\ell\cong H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)^\vee$
$T_\ell(E)=\varprojlim_n E[\ell^n]$ and $V_\ell(E)\cong H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ on the nose without any dual.
$T_\ell(E)$ is defined only for $\ell=p$ via the $p$-divisible group.
hard.explain: The Tate module is the inverse limit $T_\ell(E)=\varprojlim_n E[\ell^n]$ under the multiplication-by-$\ell$ maps — this is a free $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module of rank $2g=2$. Inverting $\ell$ gives $V_\ell(E)$, which is the dual of $H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ (or canonically $H^1$ depending on sign conventions).

questions.q: For an endomorphism $f\colon X\to X$ of a smooth projective variety with $\Gamma_f$ transverse to the diagonal, the Grothendieck–Lefschetz trace formula says $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$ equals:
choices: $\sum_i \mathrm{Tr}(f^*\mid H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))$
$\sum_i (-1)^i \mathrm{Tr}(f^*\mid H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))$
$\sum_i \dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$
$\det(f^* \mid H^*_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell))$
questions.explain: The count is an **alternating** sum of traces: odd-degree cohomology contributes with a minus sign, matching the classical topological Lefschetz formula $L(f) = \sum (-1)^i \mathrm{Tr}(f^*\mid H^i)$.

questions.q: Apply the trace formula to $X = \mathbb{P}^2_{\mathbb{F}_q}$ with $q=3$ and $f = F_q$ (geometric Frobenius), so $F^*$ acts as $q^i$ on $H^{2i}$ and odd cohomology vanishes. Compute $\#\mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{F}_3)$.
Why is transversality between $\Gamma_{F_q}$ and the diagonal automatic in characteristic $p$?
choices: Because Frobenius is finite étale.
Because $dF_q = 0$ on tangent spaces, so $T_x(\Gamma_{F_q})$ and $T_x(\Delta)$ span $T_x(X\times X)$.
Because $\mathbb{F}_q$ is a perfect field.
Transversality is never automatic; it has to be imposed.
questions.explain: The derivative of $x\mapsto x^q$ is identically zero, so the tangent space of the graph at a fixed point is just $T_x(\Delta)$'s complement inside $T_x(X\times X)$ — the two subspaces are transverse, and every fixed point contributes $+1$ to the intersection.

hard.q: Using the Lefschetz trace formula, compute $\#\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{F}_2)$. Frobenius acts as $q^k$ on $H^{2k}$, odd cohomology vanishes, and $q=2$.
hard.explain: $\#\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{F}_2)=1+q+q^2+q^3=1+2+4+8=15$, which equals $\sum_{k=0}^{3}(-1)^{2k}q^k=\sum_{k=0}^{3}q^k$ since only even-degree cohomology contributes.

hard.q: Suppose $f:X\to X$ has $\Gamma_f$ **not** transverse to $\Delta$. Which statement about applying the Lefschetz trace formula is correct?
choices: The formula $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)=\sum_i(-1)^i\mathrm{Tr}(f^*)$ still holds with the geometric intersection number replaced by a scheme-theoretic count.
The alternating trace still equals $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$ counted with multiplicities from the tangent obstruction.
The formula breaks down: the right-hand side $\sum_i(-1)^i\mathrm{Tr}(f^*)$ computes the intersection number $\Gamma_f\cdot\Delta$ (possibly $>\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$), not the naive set-theoretic count.
Non-transversality is impossible for endomorphisms of smooth projective varieties.
hard.explain: The trace formula computes the intersection number $[\Gamma_f]\cdot[\Delta]$ in $H^*(X\times X)$. Without transversality this intersection number can exceed the naive count of fixed points (each non-transverse fixed point contributes a local intersection multiplicity $>1$). Transversality (guaranteed by $dF_q=0$ for Frobenius) ensures each fixed point contributes exactly $1$.

hard.q: Which step in proving the Lefschetz trace formula for the Frobenius endomorphism uses a **cohomological** computation rather than a geometric one?
choices: Identifying $\mathbb{F}_q$-points with fixed points of the geometric Frobenius $F_q$.
Using Poincaré duality on $H^*(X\times X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ to turn $[\Gamma_f]\cdot[\Delta]$ into a trace.
Verifying that $dF_q=0$ makes $\Gamma_{F_q}$ transverse to $\Delta$.
Counting geometric points of fibres to define the degree of $F_q$.
hard.explain: The geometric step is identifying fixed points with $\mathbb{F}_q$-rational points and checking transversality. The purely cohomological step is the application of Poincaré duality on $X\times X$: it converts the intersection number $[\Gamma_f]\cdot[\Delta]$ into a trace formula via the Künneth decomposition of the diagonal class.

questions.q: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_q$ be an elliptic curve and write the Frobenius eigenvalues on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ as $\alpha, \beta$ with $\alpha\beta = q$. If $q = 5$ and $\alpha + \beta = 3$, compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_5)$.
questions.explain: $\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) = q + 1 - (\alpha+\beta) = 5 + 1 - 3 = 3$. The trace $\alpha+\beta$ is the standard $a_q$.

questions.q: For a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, Deligne's theorem says each eigenvalue $\alpha$ of Frobenius on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ has $|\alpha| = q^{i/2}$. Give one allowed eigenvalue (as a complex number $a+bi$) on $H^1$ for $q = 4$, assuming the eigenvalue lies on the positive imaginary axis.
questions.explain: On $H^1$ with $q=4$ we need $|\alpha| = \sqrt{4} = 2$. On the positive imaginary axis that is $\alpha = 2i$.

questions.q: Which statement is part of the **Weil conjectures** for a smooth projective $X/\mathbb{F}_q$ of dimension $d$?
choices: The zeta function $Z(X,t)$ is a rational function of $t$.
$Z(X,t)$ has a functional equation relating $t$ and $1/(q^d t)$.
The zeros and poles of $Z(X,t)$ lie at $|t| = q^{-i/2}$ for $0\leq i\leq 2d$ (Riemann hypothesis).
All of the above.
questions.explain: Rationality, functional equation, and the analog of the Riemann hypothesis are the three Weil conjectures (with Betti numbers matching topology a fourth). Étale cohomology with its Frobenius action is what makes them provable.

hard.q: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_3$ be an elliptic curve with Frobenius eigenvalues $\alpha,\beta$ on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ satisfying $\alpha\beta=3$ and $\alpha+\beta=-2$. Compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_9)$ using $\#E(\mathbb{F}_{q^2})=q^2+1-(\alpha^2+\beta^2)$.
For the Artin–Tate conjecture, the zeta function $Z(X,t)$ of a smooth projective curve $C/\mathbb{F}_q$ of genus $g$ factors as $Z(C,t) = \frac{P_1(t)}{(1-t)(1-qt)}$. What is $\deg P_1(t)$?
hard.explain: $P_1(t)=\det(1-tF^*\mid H^1_{\text{ét}})$ and $\dim H^1_{\text{ét}}(C,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=2g$, so $P_1$ has degree $2g$. Its roots are the reciprocals of Frobenius eigenvalues, all of absolute value $q^{1/2}$ by the Riemann hypothesis.

hard.q: Deligne's proof of the Weil Riemann hypothesis ($|\alpha_{i,j}|=q^{i/2}$) proceeds by which high-level strategy?
choices: Reduce to the one-dimensional case and apply the Hasse bound for elliptic curves directly.
Use a Lefschetz pencil to reduce to a curve fibration, then apply a monodromy argument and the hard Lefschetz theorem for $\ell$-adic sheaves.
Apply crystalline cohomology and compare Frobenius slopes with the Newton polygon.
Use the étale fundamental group to show all eigenvalues are roots of unity.
hard.explain: Deligne (Weil II, 1980) chooses a Lefschetz pencil $X\to\mathbb{P}^1$, applies the Picard–Lefschetz formula to control monodromy, invokes the hard Lefschetz theorem to upgrade a weight bound on the generic fibre, and runs an induction on dimension. The key input is that the local monodromy operators are unipotent, which forces the eigenvalues of Frobenius to have the correct absolute values.

questions.q: Artin's comparison theorem states that for a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{C}$ and any prime $\ell$:
choices: $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Q})\otimes\mathbb{Q}_\ell$
$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \cong H^i_{\text{dR}}(X/\mathbb{C})$
$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = 0$ for $i>0$
$H^i_{\text{ét}}$ depends nontrivially on $\ell$
questions.explain: Artin's theorem: étale cohomology of a complex variety agrees with topological singular cohomology after tensoring to $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$. In particular $\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ is the classical Betti number $b_i$, independent of $\ell$.

questions.q: Using the comparison theorem, compute the $\ell$-adic Betti number $b_2 = \dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^2_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$ over $\mathbb{C}$.
questions.explain: By Künneth and comparison, $H^2(\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1) = H^2(\mathbb{P}^1)\otimes H^0(\mathbb{P}^1) \oplus H^0\otimes H^2$, giving $b_2 = 1+1 = 2$. The two classes are the pullbacks of the hyperplane class from each factor.

questions.q: Over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$, why can we NOT use $\ell = p$ in $\ell$-adic cohomology, and what replaces it?
choices: Nothing is wrong; $\ell=p$ works fine.
$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_p)$ is pathological; crystalline cohomology $H^i_{\text{cris}}(X/W(k))$ is the correct $p$-adic Weil cohomology.
One uses singular cohomology of $X(\mathbb{C})$ instead.
One uses de Rham cohomology of the generic fiber.
questions.explain: Étale cohomology with $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$ coefficients sees Frobenius-kernel pathology in characteristic $p$. Crystalline cohomology (Berthelot–Grothendieck) replaces it, producing a $W(k)$-module with semi-linear Frobenius whose characteristic polynomial matches the $\ell$-adic ones for $\ell\neq p$.

hard.q: Artin's comparison isomorphism $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z}/n)$ requires $X$ to be defined over $\mathbb{C}$. Which step in the proof is the key algebraic–topological input?
choices: Showing that every étale cover of $X$ comes from a topological cover of $X(\mathbb{C})$ via the analytification functor.
Applying the proper base-change theorem to reduce to a finite field.
Invoking the Mayer–Vietoris sequence for Zariski open covers.
Using the comparison between de Rham and Dolbeault cohomology.
hard.explain: The core of Artin's theorem is that analytification gives an equivalence between finite étale covers of $X$ (in the algebraic category) and finite topological covers of $X(\mathbb{C})$. This makes the étale site over $\mathbb{C}$ equivalent to the site of locally contractible opens on the analytic space, from which the comparison of cohomology with $\mathbb{Z}/n$ coefficients follows.

hard.q: Let $X$ be a smooth projective K3 surface over $\mathbb{C}$. The singular Betti numbers are $b_0=b_4=1$, $b_1=b_3=0$, $b_2=22$. Using the comparison theorem, compute $\chi_{\ell}(X)=\sum_i(-1)^i\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$.
hard.explain: $\chi_{\ell}(X)=b_0-b_1+b_2-b_3+b_4=1-0+22-0+1=24$. By comparison with singular cohomology, $\chi_{\ell}$ equals the topological Euler characteristic $\chi(X(\mathbb{C}))=24$ for any $\ell$.

hard.q: Which statement correctly describes a **failure** of the comparison theorem outside its hypotheses?
choices: For a smooth proper variety over $\mathbb{Q}_p$, $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X_{\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_p},\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ with $\ell\neq p$ can have dimension different from the complex Betti number.
For $X$ smooth projective over $\mathbb{C}$, the dimensions $\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ depend on $\ell$.

For $X$ a smooth quasi-projective (non-proper) complex variety, the comparison $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Q})\otimes\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ can fail to be an isomorphism.
The comparison theorem requires $\ell=2$; for odd primes it is only an injection.
hard.explain: For smooth **projective** varieties over $\mathbb{C}$ the comparison holds in all degrees. For non-proper (quasi-projective) varieties it can fail: étale cohomology with constant coefficients $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ computes the cohomology of a singular complex, which need not agree with singular cohomology in high degrees (one needs compactly supported or mixed Hodge-theoretic corrections). The theorem in its clean form requires properness.
